---
output:
  html_document: default
  pdf_document: default
---
<br> 
<center><img src="https://i.imgur.com/AbCCpQO.png" width="700"></center>


_____

<a id='TOC'></a>


# Text Data 
_____


Text can also be visualized, but unlike numeric variables, text usually needs extra preparation before plotting. Words appear in different forms, some words are very common but not very informative, and some meaningful ideas are expressed as phrases rather than single words.

In this session, you will learn a simple workflow for visualizing text data. We will begin with word frequencies, then compare different graphical options:

* word clouds
* ranked bar charts
* lollipop charts
* treemaps
* topic models
* co-occurrence networks

The goal is not only to produce attractive plots, but also to understand what each plot is good for.


1. Get the text:

Let me get a data frame with texts from some tweets:

```{r}
# clean memory
rm(list = ls()) 
link1="https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/basicText/"
link2="raw/main/trumps.csv"

trumpLink=paste0(link1,link2)
allTweets=read.csv(trumpLink ,stringsAsFactors = F)
```

The data has these columns:

```{r}
names(allTweets)
```
The Twitter text column has this information:

```{r}
head(allTweets$text,2)
```



2. Select the observations of interest

This data frame has several columns that allow subsetting. In this example, I will keep only tweets that are not retweets:

```{r}

SomeTrumpTweets = allTweets[allTweets$is_retweet == FALSE, ]
SomeTrumpTweets = SomeTrumpTweets[, c("created_at", "text")]
# see
head(SomeTrumpTweets, 3)

```

At this point, we still have full text messages. To visualize text, we first need to break each message into smaller units.


3. Turn text into words

This process is called **tokenization**. Here, each tweet will be split into individual words.


```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
library(dplyr)
library(tidytext) 

WordsIn_SomeTrumpTweets = SomeTrumpTweets |>
                 unnest_tokens(output=EachWord, # column created
                               input=text,# input column from SomeTrumpTweets
                               token="words") # level of unnesting

# the date is the same for all the words coming from the
# same tweet:
head(WordsIn_SomeTrumpTweets,10) # notice 'EachWord'

```

Notice that the date is repeated for every word that comes from the same tweet.

How many word-tokens do we have now?

```{r}
nrow(WordsIn_SomeTrumpTweets) # count of words
```
This number includes repeated words, which is useful because frequency is often our first summary of text.


4. Remove common words: **stopwords**

Many very frequent words carry little substantive meaning. Words such as the, and, of, or to are useful for grammar, but not always useful for analysis. These are called _stopwords_.

```{r}
# calling the file 
data(stop_words) # from library 'tidytext'
# seeing some 'STOP WORDS'  
head(stop_words)
```

Then, we  remove the stop words from the _EachWord_ column:

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(dplyr)

 # The column 'word' from 'stop_words' will be compared # to the column 'EachWord' in 'WordsIn_SomeTrumpTweets' 
WordsIn_SomeTrumpTweets = WordsIn_SomeTrumpTweets |>anti_join(stop_words,
                                             by = c("EachWord" = "word"))

# You have these many rows now:

nrow(WordsIn_SomeTrumpTweets) # less than 50%

```

After removing stopwords, the number of rows decreases because many very common words are filtered out.

This does not mean the remaining words are **per se** important. It only means we are removing words that are usually too general to help interpretation.


5. Compute **frequency** of each word:

Here, you are simply producing a frequency table:

```{r}
forCloud = WordsIn_SomeTrumpTweets |>
  count(EachWord, name = "Counts", sort = TRUE)

head(forCloud, 10)
```

The most frequent words appear at the top.



Since you have a frequency table, you could prepare a barplot:

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(data=forCloud) + geom_bar(aes(x=reorder(EachWord,Counts), y=Counts), stat = 'identity') + coord_flip()
```

 

However, text from tweets often contains noisy elements such as shortened links or platform-specific tokens. So some additional cleaning may still be necessary.
 
```{r}

badWords = c("https", "t.co")

forCloud = forCloud |>
  filter(!EachWord %in% badWords) |>  # dropping by text:
  filter(Counts > 4) # dropping by count:

head(forCloud, 10)

```
 
6. Ranked bar chart

Let's re create the  bar chart, just for the top 15 rows.

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(forCloud |> slice_head(n = 15),
       aes(x = reorder(EachWord, Counts), y = Counts)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Count",
       title = "Most frequent words")
```
This plot is very useful because it allows precise comparison. You can clearly see:

* which words are most frequent
* their ranking
* the size of the differences between them

> Notice that for comparing frequencies, bar charts are usually better than word clouds.

7. Lollipop chart

A lollipop chart shows the same information as a bar chart, but with a lighter appearance.

```{r}
ggplot(forCloud |> slice_head(n = 15),
       aes(x = reorder(EachWord, Counts), y = Counts)) +
  geom_segment(aes(xend = EachWord, y = 0, yend = Counts)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Count",
       title = "Most frequent words: lollipop chart")
```
This is useful when you want a cleaner look but still need a ranked comparison.



8. Word cloud

Now let us build the word cloud using **ggwordcloud**.

```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=TRUE, message=FALSE}
library(ggwordcloud)

ggplot(forCloud,
       aes(label = EachWord,
           size = Counts,
           color = Counts)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud_area() +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "red", high = "darkred")

```

You can improve this using **scale_size_area()**:
```{r}
ggplot(forCloud,
       aes(label = EachWord,
           size = Counts,
           color = Counts)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud_area(eccentricity = 0.65) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 13) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "red", high = "darkred")
```



Word clouds are often visually attractive, but they are not ideal for accurate comparison. They are better for quick engagement and general impression than for careful measurement.

9. Treemap

A treemap is another way to visualize frequencies using area.


```{r}
library(treemapify)

ggplot(forCloud %>% slice_head(n = 20),
       aes(area = Counts, fill = Counts, label = EachWord)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_text(place = "centre", colour = "white") +
  labs(title = "Word frequencies as a treemap")
```

Treemaps are compact and visually appealing, but again, they are not as precise as bars for comparing values.

10. A second example: reading from a TXT file

Not all text data comes from tweets. Sometimes you simply have a text file.

Suppose we have this file:


![](https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/basicText/blob/main/sometext.png?raw=true)


You can simply save that text as a **txt** file. Let me work with that file:

```{r, warning=FALSE}
LinkText="https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/basicText/raw/main/sometext.txt"
otherText = read.delim(LinkText, header = FALSE)

## see some
head(otherText)
```

Notice that a new row is created whenever a new paragraph or line break is found.

Now let us tokenize the text and remove stopwords:

```{r}
otherText_words=otherText |>
               unnest_tokens(output=EachWord,
                             input=V1,# column of the texts
                             token="words") |>
               anti_join(stop_words,
                         by = c("EachWord" = "word"))
# you get
head(otherText_words,20)


```
Now compute frequencies:

```{r}

txtAsc_descending = otherText_words %>%
  count(EachWord, name = "Counts", sort = TRUE) %>%
  filter(Counts > 4)

# you have
head(txtAsc_descending, 10)

```

11. Plotting the TXT example

Word cloud

```{r}

ggplot(txtAsc_descending,
       aes(label = EachWord,
           size = Counts,
           color = Counts)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud() +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 10) +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "red", high = "darkred")
```

Barplots

```{r}
ggplot(txtAsc_descending %>% slice_head(n = 15),
       aes(x = reorder(EachWord, Counts), y = Counts)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Count",
       title = "Most frequent words in the text")
```

Or, a little better:

```{r}
top15 = txtAsc_descending %>% slice_head(n = 15)

ggplot(top15,
       aes(x = reorder(EachWord, Counts), y = Counts)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Count") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(100 * Counts / sum(Counts), 1), "%")),
            nudge_y = 2)
```
This is often more informative than a word cloud because the audience can see both counts and proportions directly.


12. Co-occurrence networks

So far, we have treated words as isolated units. But language also has structure: some words tend to appear together.

A simple way to explore this is through bigrams, that is, pairs of words that appear next to each other.

```{r}
library(tidyr)
library(igraph)
library(ggraph)

bigrams = SomeTrumpTweets %>%
  unnest_tokens(bigram, text, token = "ngrams", n = 2)

bigram_counts = bigrams %>%
  separate(bigram, into = c("word1", "word2"), sep = " ") %>%
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word,
         !word2 %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  count(word1, word2, sort = TRUE) %>%
  filter(n >= 5)

head(bigram_counts, 10)
```

```{r}
bigram_graph = graph_from_data_frame(bigram_counts)

ggraph(bigram_graph, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = n), alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_node_point(size = 4) +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name), repel = TRUE) +
  theme_void()
```

This plot does not focus on word frequency alone. Instead, it helps reveal relationships between words.

That is often more meaningful than treating words separately.


13. Topic models

Sometimes we want to move from individual words to broader themes in a collection of documents. Topic models help us do that.

The basic idea is simple: instead of asking “Which words are frequent?”, topic models ask:

Which words tend to appear together across documents, suggesting a latent topic?

Here, each tweet is treated as a document.

```{r}
library(topicmodels)

tweet_words = SomeTrumpTweets %>%
  mutate(doc_id = row_number()) %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text) %>%
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word")

tweet_dtm = tweet_words %>%
  count(doc_id, word) %>%
  cast_dtm(document = doc_id, term = word, value = n)

lda_model = LDA(tweet_dtm, k = 3, control = list(seed = 1234))
```

Let us extract the most important terms in each topic:
```{r}
library(tidytext)

topics_top_terms = tidy(lda_model, matrix = "beta") %>%
  group_by(topic) %>%
  slice_max(beta, n = 8) %>%
  ungroup()
```

To plot them nicely, we use a helper from tidytext:
```{r}
library(tidytext)

ggplot(topics_top_terms,
       aes(x = reorder_within(term, beta, topic),
           y = beta)) +
  geom_col() +
  facet_wrap(~topic, scales = "free") +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "", y = "Beta",
       title = "Top terms by topic")
```
This is not just a visualization technique. It is also a modeling strategy for discovering themes in text.

14. Interpreting text plots carefully

Text graphics can be attractive, but interpretation requires caution.

A frequent word is not necessarily an important idea. It may be:

- a repeated name
- a slogan
- a response to current events
- part of criticism or support
- a word used sarcastically

So, frequency does not automatically imply meaning.

Also, different forms of the same idea may appear as different words. For example:

- run
- runs
- running

This is why more advanced text analysis often uses **stemming** or **lemmatization**.

Single-word plots are a useful starting point, but they do not capture all the complexity of language.

15. What each plot is good for

Here is a useful summary:

* Ranked bar chart: best for precise comparison
* Lollipop chart: similar to bars, visually lighter
* Word cloud: engaging, but weak for exact comparison
* Treemap: compact view of relative frequency
* Co-occurrence network: reveals relationships between words
* Topic model plot: reveals broader themes across documents 

So the choice of plot depends on the goal.

- If your goal is comparison, bars are usually best.

- If your goal is engagement, a word cloud may help.

- If your goal is structure or themes, networks and topic models are more useful.

16. Final remark

Text visualization is powerful, but language is complex.

Good text graphics require:

* good preprocessing
* good visualization choices
* careful interpretation

In other words, text visualization is not only about making words look attractive. It is about transforming language into data without forgetting that language always carries context, ambiguity, and meaning.
